question:
Will Bitcoin dip to $90,000 by December 31, 2025?

Description: This market will immediately resolve to "Yes" if any Binance 1 minute candle for Bitcoin (BTCUSDT) between October 2, 2025, 11:00 and December 31, 2025, 23:59 in the ET timezone has a final "Low" price of $90,000 or lower. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No."

The resolution source for this market is Binance, specifically the BTCUSDT "Low" prices available at https://www.binance.com/en/trade/BTC_USDT, with the chart settings on "1m" for one-minute candles selected on the top bar.

Please note that the outcome of this market depends solely on the price data from the Binance BTCUSDT trading pair. Prices from other exchanges, different trading pairs, or spot markets will not be considered for the resolution of this market.

evidences.title: Will Bitcoin Price Drop Below $90,000 as Key Psychological Support Fails?
evidences.content: Using the 2025 peak near $125,000, a typical pullback would place Bitcoin between $75,000 and $93,000. These drawdown levels align closely with current technical and on-chain floors. As a result, analysts see three major zones forming. ## Key Bitcoin Price Levels To Watch The first support sits at $92,000 to $95,000, matching the 6–12 month cost basis and recent ETF inflow levels. This area is likely the first reaction point. However, a stronger correction could push Bitcoin into the $85,000 to $90,000 band, which aligns with a standard 25%–30% mid-cycle decline. The bearish scenario extends deeper. If ETF outflows accelerate and macro conditions worsen, Bitcoin could retest the $75,000 to $82,000 zone.

evidences.title: Bitcoin Q1 2025: Historic Highs, Volatility, and Institutional ...
evidences.content: Macro dynamics significantly influenced ETF flows. The market downturn between February and April 2025—driven by regulatory uncertainty, miner selling pressure, and broader risk-off sentiment—led to net outflows from several providers. Notably, Franklin Templeton and WisdomTree experienced steady declines, with WisdomTree’s holdings dropping sharply from 11,587 BTC in December 2024 to 1,548 BTC in April 2025, reflecting retail investor exits amid heightened volatility and competitive pressures from larger ETFs. Valkyrie similarly shrank from peaks around 7,591 BTC in early 2024 to about 2,991 BTC by April 2025. [...] ### Gini Coefficient From January to April 2025, Bitcoin’s Gini coefficient—a measure of wealth concentration within the network—showed modest upward momentum, rising from 0.4675 in January to roughly 0.4677 by April. While this indicates only a slight increase in concentration, it’s notable following the similarly mild but consistent rise from late 2024. In Q4 2024, the coefficient edged upward from 0.4657 in December to 0.4667, coinciding with Bitcoin’s historic surge above $100,000, spurred by bullish macro catalysts including the U.S. election outcome, Fed polic...

evidences.content: ### Projected Minimum and Maximum Values Forecasting exact price points is challenging, but many analyses within the Bitcoin price analysis 2025 outlook point toward significant upward potential. For 2025, some projections place the minimum expected value around $113,245.54, with the maximum potentially reaching $119,345.02. These figures are based on various technical indicators and market sentiment analyses. The average trading price is often cited in the range of $125,444. This outlook is influenced by several factors, including the ongoing adoption of Bitcoin ETFs and the anticipation surrounding future market cycles. evidences.title: 2026 Outlook: The End of the Four-Year Cycle
evidences.content: The mathematics are unambiguous: institutional flows have superseded mining supply as the marginal price driver. When ETFs are buying, prices rise regardless of mining output. When ETFs are selling, prices fall regardless of the halving's supply constraint. The halving cycle is over. The institutional flow cycle has begun. This article synthesizes the full Amberdata Crypto Market Review 2025 analysis into a 2026 outlook: the current market setup, scenario analysis with probability-weighted outcomes, the watchlist indicators that will signal regime changes, and the catalysts that could accelerate or derail each scenario. As context for this outlook, the full Amberdata Crypto Market Review 2025 and 2026 Outlook identifies six distinct market regimes: [...] Regulatory Catalysts: Policy decisions unlock or restrict capital pools. 401(k) access could dwarf historical ETF volumes Macro Conditions: Risk-on vs risk-off positioning affects institutional allocation independent of Bitcoin-specific factors ETF Flow Projections. 2026 forecasts range $15-50B vs $23B actual in 2025. Galaxy most bullish; Citi conservative.Figure 14.3: ETF Flow Projections. 2026 forecasts range $15-50B vs $23B a...

evidences.title: Bitcoin Price Drawdown from ATH Chart | Newhedge
evidences.content: ### Historical Drawdowns and Current Cycle Comparisons In previous Bitcoin market cycles, the percentage drawdown has varied significantly: Cycle 1 (2010 - 2013) - Peak: December 2013 - Drawdown: 93% from ATH Cycle 2 (2014 to 2017) • Peak: December 2017 • Drawdown: ~86-87% from ATH Cycle 3 (2018 to 2021) • Peak: November 2021 • Drawdown: ~75-77% from ATH ### Spikes and Cooldown Phases: A Pattern to Watch One of the most intriguing aspects of Bitcoin’s historical drawdowns is the tendency for spikes in price recovery during the bear market before cooling off again. For instance: • In 2015, Bitcoin experienced a spike that saw the drawdown reduce to just 32% below its ATH before cooling off for several months. [...] • In 2019, Bitcoin similarly spiked, reducing the drawdown to about 34% below ATH, followed by a long cooldown period lasting up to nine months. Tracking Bitcoin’s percentage drawdown from its all-time high provides valuable context for understanding where the market stands in the broader cycle. While it isn’t a perfect predictor of future price action, it offers several key insights: Historical Context: By comparing current drawdowns with previous cycles, investors ca...

evidences.title: Post Halving – Bitcoin Miners Landscape - AMINA Bank
evidences.content: ### Section 3.1: Operational Adjustments The 2024 halving significantly impacted the mining industry, with difficulty increases in the months leading up to the event as miners maximized rewards before the cut. Figure 9: BTC Hashrate Source: AMINA Bank, Glassnode (4 September 2025) ### Hedging Bitcoin’s volatility averages 8x that of high yield bonds and 4x that of domestic equities, making hedging critical for mining operations. Figure 10: Bitcoin Volatility Source: AMINA Bank, Glassnode (4 September 2025) Miners use derivatives to protect against volatility, including forward swaps to lock in prices above spot and put options for minimum price protection. These transactions enable pre-set profitability levels and cash flow certainty. [...] Hedged miners may benefit from price drawdowns, as hedges offset price falls while potentially increasing market share if hashrate declines. Sharp increase in BTC futures open interest around halving suggest hedging activities by sophisticated players. Figure 11: BTC Options Open Interest on Major Exchanges Source: AMINA Bak, Glassnode (4 September 2025) ### Turning off inefficient rigs Post-halving, simple measures of revenue indicate the mi...

evidences.content: `params` `json` `import requests url = ' params = { 'symbol': 'BTCUSDT', 'interval': '1h' } response = requests.get(url, params=params) print(response.json())` carkod's user avatar collect data into dataframe from start date to end date [...] Question Edited: The output that I am getting is: {"code":-1102,"msg":"Mandatory parameter 'symbol' was not sent, was empty/null, or malformed."} Brian Tompsett - 汤莱恩's user avatar Jin Barai's user avatar `<Response >` ## 3 Answers 3 you are missing the mandatory parameters symbol and interval, the query should be like this: you need to import only requests: `import requests market = 'BTCUSDT' tick_interval = '1h' url = ' data = requests.get(url).json() print(data)` Please check the official Binance REST API documentation here: Domenico's user avatar The requests python package has a `params`, `json` argument, so you don't need to import any of those packages you are importing. evidences.title: Binance Fee Overview
evidences.content: Image 1: Binance Square Login Sign Up DiscoverNewsNotificationProfileBookmarksChatsHistoryCreator CenterSettings Article Binance Fee Overview Image 2 Image 3 Elon Jamess Jun 8, 2025 Follow Report Block User 1. Exchange Fees Spot Trading Standard fees are 0.10% for both makers and takers. If you opt to pay using BNB, you receive a 25% discount, bringing rates down to 0.075% . Futures Trading (USDⓈ‑M & COIN‑M) Maker fees start at 0.02%, with taker fees at 0.04%–0.05% for VIP 0 users. Paying with BNB on USDⓈ‑M yields a 10% discount, so taker fees drop to 0.045% . Other Trading Includes options, margin, P2P, convert, and NFT markets—each with its own structure. For instance, options take 0.03% for both maker and taker, and spot margin fees mirror standard spot rates . [...] 2. Deposit & Withdrawal Fees Deposits Crypto deposits are free, except for small burns on specific tokens (e.g., Terra Classic). Fiat deposit fees vary: SEPA is ~€1 flat, card payments ~1–3%, depending on the region and method . Withdrawals Fees vary based on crypto and network. For example, Bitcoin withdrawal costs 0.0002 BTC (minimum 0.0004 BTC). USDT on TRC20 is around 1 USDT, while ERC20 networks are pricier...

evidences.title: Binance Futures Fee Structure & Fee Calculations
evidences.content: Position Value = Contract Size execution price For example, a Regular User’s maker fee is 0.02% and a Regular User’s taker fee is 0.05% The Regular User buys 1 BTC worth of BTCUSDT USDS-M Perpetual Futures Contract by making a Market Order: Position Value = Contract Size execution price = 1 BTC 10,104 = 10,104 USDT Taker fee is paid: 10,104 0.05% = 5.052 USDT After the price rises, the Regular User sells 1 BTC worth of BTCUSDT USDS-M Perpetual Futures C using a Limit Order: Position Value = Contract Size execution price = 1 BTC 11,104 = 11,104 USDT Maker fee is paid: 11,104 0.02% = 2.2208 USDT Disclaimer: [...] Maker fee: 0.09 0.02% = 0.000018 BTC _Disclaimer:_ _Trading fees shown are calculated according to the hypothetical fee rates. Capitalised terms used in the above calculation are defined in the__Clearing Procedures__. Fees are subject to change by Binance in accordance with the__Terms of Use__, the__Exchange Rules__(including the__Exchange Procedures__) and the__Clearing Rules__(including the__Clearing Procedures__)._ _For the latest fee schedule, please refer to the__Fee Rate Table (COIN-M Futures)__. All trading involves risk and fees will be charged as applicable based...

evidences.title: Binance Fees 2025: Trading, Deposit & Withdrawal Costs - Coinspot.io
evidences.content: Makers, who post limit orders that add liquidity, often pay less than takers, whose orders execute immediately and remove liquidity . Fee tiers are determined by rolling 30-day turnover. Increasing volume can move a user into higher VIP levels, usually reducing maker/taker rates. Promotions may temporarily lower costs through discounts or cashback programs. Holding BNB can also reduce some fees in eligible categories. Use the next sections to map fee components across each product and compare likely costs for different trading styles. ## Binance Spot Trading Fees For regular accounts, spot maker and taker rates start at 0.1%. If trading USDC pairs, makers remain at the standard rate while takers can be slightly lower at 0.095%. [...] To use it, keep some BNB in the Spot wallet and enable the BNB deduction feature. The settings can be toggled in two places: Option One: On the Spot Trading page, open Fee Level and switch the BNB/USDT deduction on or off. Option Two: In the Fees & Transactions Overview, toggle the BNB Discount setting on or off. After activation, Binance draws fees from the BNB balance whenever eligible, so discounted rates apply automatically when conditions are m...

evidences.title: Volatility in Bitcoin ETF Flows - Ecoinometrics
evidences.content: The takeaway is that investors should not expect a fast or well-defined easing path from the Fed in early 2026. Policy remains slow, data-dependent, and lacking strong conviction. For Bitcoin, this backdrop is not automatically negative. What matters more than policy clarity is liquidity. As long as financial conditions remain loose, the absence of firm Fed conviction does not translate into immediate downside risk. And so far, conditions have improved. The National Financial Conditions Index has trended lower again in recent weeks, indicating that markets are not experiencing tightening stress. This means financial conditions are not currently a headwind for Bitcoin. They allow capital to move into risk-on assets, even if they are not yet acting as a strong tailwind. [...] Mixed Signals From the Federal Reserve Viewed together, today’s charts describe a market where the building blocks for a sustained move are not yet aligned. Liquidity is available, Bitcoin continues to dominate crypto capital, but demand remains episodic and policy signals remain mixed. The result is a market biased toward stabilization rather than acceleration. In case you missed it, here are the other topic...
